title: Falls — Older Adult
oneLiner: A fall in an older adult is a *symptom* of underlying risk until proven otherwise — a leading cause of injury, loss of independence, and preventable death.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Intracranial haemorrhage (especially on anticoagulation):** focal neurology, GCS drop, vomiting — CT head stat regardless of anticoagulant type. Single sign: *any acute change in GCS or new focal weakness after a fall*.
- **Cervical spine injury with cord involvement:** neck pain, midline tenderness, limb weakness or sensory level — full spinal precautions, immobilise. Single sign: *tenderness over C-spine or neurological deficit*.
- **Acute coronary syndrome / arrhythmia causing the fall (misdiagnosed as mechanical fall):** if patient denies tripping and has no mechanical cause, consider syncope — do an ECG before attributing to "simple fall". Single sign: *witnessed loss of consciousness or pre-syncope*.
- **Rhabdomyolysis from prolonged lie (≥1 hour on the floor):** risk of acute kidney injury (AKI) and hyperkalemia. Single sign: *creatine kinase (CK) >5000 U/L and myoglobinuria*.
sections.Presentation:
- Cardinal symptoms: found on the floor after a fall (witnessed or unwitnessed), history of "legs gave way", "I tripped", or "I don't remember". Often accompanied by:
  - Inability to get up unassisted
  - Hip or wrist pain (fracture)
  - Headache, confusion, or drowsiness (head injury)
  - Fear of falling again
- Key exam findings:
  - Full set of vital signs including lying and standing BP (postural hypotension)
  - Head-to-toe inspection for bruising, lacerations, bony deformity
  - Neurological exam (GCS, limb power, cranial nerves)
  - Spinal tenderness and rectal tone if concern for cord injury
  - Acute functional decline (e.g., cannot walk after fall)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Mechanical fall (trip/slip)** → clear external cause, no LOC, patient recalls event.
- **Syncope (cardiac / vasovagal)** → no clear trigger, brief LOC, rapid recovery ± prodromal symptoms (palpitations, chest pain, lightheadedness).
- **Stroke / TIA** → focal neurological signs (weakness, facial droop, speech), sudden onset ± fall as first presentation.
- **Septic / dehydrated** → fever, hypotension, confusion, raised inflammatory markers.
- **Hypoglycaemia** → sudden confusion, sweating, pallor — check capillary glucose.
- **Seizure** → witnessed convulsions, tongue biting, incontinence, post-ictal confusion.
- **Vestibular / peripheral neuropathy** → dizziness/disequilibrium without true LOC.
- **Medication side effect** (e.g., postural hypotension from alpha-blockers, sedatives) → temporal relationship.

**Splitter:** Did the patient lose consciousness? Yes → **syncope workup** (cardiac/prodrome). No → **mechanical fall workup** (extrinsic/intrinsic risk factors). If unwitnessed, assume syncope until proven otherwise.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - 12-lead ECG (looking for AF, heart block, LVH, long QT, acute ischaemia)
  - Capillary blood glucose
  - Orthostatic vitals (lying + standing at 1 and 3 minutes — drop in SBP ≥20 mmHg or DBP ≥10 mmHg)
  - Urinalysis (infection)
  - GCS and baseline cognitive screen (AMTS / 4AT)
- **Bloods (urgent):**
  - FBC, U&E, CRP, glucose, CK (if down time >1 hour)
  - Troponin (if chest pain, SOB, or syncope)
  - Consider thyroid function and vitamin B12 if first presentation of cognitive impairment
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** CT head if on anticoagulation / antiplatelet, GCS <15, focal neurology, witnessed LOC, or >65 with diffuse headache (NICE CG176)
  - **Second-line:** C-spine XR (if no high-risk mechanism and normal neuro exam) or CT C-spine (if high-risk or inadequate XR). Follow [local guidelines for clearance]
- **Special tests:**
  - ECG telemetry if suspected arrhythmia (monitor for ≥24h)
  - Carotid sinus massage only under specialist guidance
  - Echocardiogram if syncope with murmur / LVH / arrhythmia
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - NICE fall-risk assessment: ≥65 with one or more falls in past year → full multifactorial assessment
- **Never-miss test:** **ECG** — every fall in an older adult, even if mechanical cause suspected. A normal ECG does not rule out arrhythmia, but an abnormal one changes management immediately.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **ABC assessment** — manage airway if GCS <8, immobilise C-spine if any concern
  - **Treat injuries:** analgesia for fractures; wound care for lacerations; sutures/steri-strips if needed
  - **IV fluids** if hypotensive, septic, or prolonged lie (1L 0.9% saline stat, then reassess)
  - **Reverse anticoagulation** if confirmed intracranial haemorrhage — prothrombin complex concentrate or idarucizumab per [local protocol]
  - **Admit** if head injury on anticoagulation, new fracture requiring surgery, GCS <15, or unable to mobilise safely
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - **Full multifactorial falls assessment:** history of falls, gait/balance, muscle strength, home hazards, feet/footwear, vision, medications review
  - **Postural blood pressures** — repeat morning and evening for 3 days
  - **Bone protection:** check vitamin D and calcium; prescribe bisphosphonate if osteoporotic fracture (see Drugs)
  - **Hip protectors** if high risk of further falls with hip fracture
  - **Physiotherapy review** for mobility aid and home safety assessment
  - **Referral to falls clinic** or geriatrician for ongoing management
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Community falls prevention programme (strength and balance exercises)
  - Home hazard reduction (e.g., grab rails, non-slip mats, high-seat chairs)
  - Review all medications — stop or reduce sedatives, antihypertensives if postural hypotension
  - Vitamin D and calcium supplementation if deficient
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** 1L 0.9% saline/8h then reassess (or as per renal function)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40mg SC once daily) if immobile and no contraindication
  - **Diet:** full diet unless concern for swallowing; keep well hydrated
  - **Observations:** NEWS 4-hourly initially; after 24h can reduce to 8-hourly if stable
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line for fracture prevention:**
  - **Alendronic acid** 70mg PO once weekly (take with full glass of water, sit upright for ≥30 min after dose)
  - **Calcium + vitamin D** (e.g., Adcal D3 1 tab twice daily) — prescribe with any bisphosphonate
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Risedronate 35mg PO once weekly [check local policy]
  - Denosumab 60mg SC every 6 months (if intolerant to bisphosphonates)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Avoid bisphosphonates in eGFR <30, hypocalcaemia, oesophageal stricture
  - Do not give oral bisphosphonate with food, iron, calcium, or coffee within 30 min
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Alendronic acid: not recommended if CrCl <35 mL/min
  - Denosumab: no renal dose reduction, but monitor calcium closely in severe renal impairment
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Bisphosphonates** — giving with food or bedtime; can cause severe oesophagitis. Remind patient: “Swallow whole with plain water first thing in morning, sit up for 30 min, no breakfast yet.”
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Hip fracture (most common serious injury)
  - Intracranial haemorrhage (especially if on anticoagulants)
  - Cervical spine fracture
  - Rhabdomyolysis (AKI, hyperkalemia, cardiac arrest)
  - Wound infection / pressure sores from prolonged lie
- **Chronic:**
  - Fear of falling → activity restriction → deconditioning → further falls
  - Loss of independence → institutionalisation
  - Osteoporotic fractures (hip, wrist, vertebral)
sections.Prognosis: - 30% of older adults who fall in hospital will fall again within 6 months. 20% of hip fracture patients die within 1 year. Early multifactorial assessment reduces fall risk by 25%.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Check orthostatic vitals** on every older adult who falls — simple, cheap, and reveals a treatable cause.
- **Always ask “did you trip or lose consciousness?”** — if unclear, assume syncope and do a cardiac workup.
- **Review the medication chart** — especially antihypertensives, sedatives, anticholinergics — and consider deprescribing.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not label a fall as “mechanical”** without ruling out syncope — missing an arrhythmia or TIA is a missed opportunity for secondary prevention.
- **Do not assume a normal CT head rules out subdural** — acute subdurals can evolve over hours to days; repeat CT if GCS drops.
- **Do not send patient home** without checking safety — if they cannot walk independently, they need supervision; arrange a home care package first.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - New or worsening headache, confusion, or vomiting (suggests delayed intracranial bleed)
  - Unable to weight-bear or severe pain (undiagnosed fracture)
  - Any further fall within 24 hours
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP within 1 week for medication review and falls assessment
  - Outpatient falls clinic referral (if available) for comprehensive balance and strength programme
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / crash team if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (or single parameter ≥3)
  - GCS drop by ≥2 points or new focal neurology
  - Hypotension unresponsive to 1L bolus (MAP <65)
  - ECG showing new arrhythmia (AF with rapid rate, heart block, VT)
  - Concern for spinal cord compression (sensory level, acute urinary retention, loss of rectal tone)

rawMarkdown:
## Falls — Older Adult
**One-liner:** A fall in an older adult is a *symptom* of underlying risk until proven otherwise — a leading cause of injury, loss of independence, and preventable death.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Intracranial haemorrhage (especially on anticoagulation):** focal neurology, GCS drop, vomiting — CT head stat regardless of anticoagulant type. Single sign: *any acute change in GCS or new focal weakness after a fall*.
- **Cervical spine injury with cord involvement:** neck pain, midline tenderness, limb weakness or sensory level — full spinal precautions, immobilise. Single sign: *tenderness over C-spine or neurological deficit*.
- **Acute coronary syndrome / arrhythmia causing the fall (misdiagnosed as mechanical fall):** if patient denies tripping and has no mechanical cause, consider syncope — do an ECG before attributing to "simple fall". Single sign: *witnessed loss of consciousness or pre-syncope*.
- **Rhabdomyolysis from prolonged lie (≥1 hour on the floor):** risk of acute kidney injury (AKI) and hyperkalemia. Single sign: *creatine kinase (CK) >5000 U/L and myoglobinuria*.

### Presentation
- Cardinal symptoms: found on the floor after a fall (witnessed or unwitnessed), history of "legs gave way", "I tripped", or "I don't remember". Often accompanied by:
  - Inability to get up unassisted
  - Hip or wrist pain (fracture)
  - Headache, confusion, or drowsiness (head injury)
  - Fear of falling again
- Key exam findings:
  - Full set of vital signs including lying and standing BP (postural hypotension)
  - Head-to-toe inspection for bruising, lacerations, bony deformity
  - Neurological exam (GCS, limb power, cranial nerves)
  - Spinal tenderness and rectal tone if concern for cord injury
  - Acute functional decline (e.g., cannot walk after fall)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Mechanical fall (trip/slip)** → clear external cause, no LOC, patient recalls event.
- **Syncope (cardiac / vasovagal)** → no clear trigger, brief LOC, rapid recovery ± prodromal symptoms (palpitations, chest pain, lightheadedness).
- **Stroke / TIA** → focal neurological signs (weakness, facial droop, speech), sudden onset ± fall as first presentation.
- **Septic / dehydrated** → fever, hypotension, confusion, raised inflammatory markers.
- **Hypoglycaemia** → sudden confusion, sweating, pallor — check capillary glucose.
- **Seizure** → witnessed convulsions, tongue biting, incontinence, post-ictal confusion.
- **Vestibular / peripheral neuropathy** → dizziness/disequilibrium without true LOC.
- **Medication side effect** (e.g., postural hypotension from alpha-blockers, sedatives) → temporal relationship.

**Splitter:** Did the patient lose consciousness? Yes → **syncope workup** (cardiac/prodrome). No → **mechanical fall workup** (extrinsic/intrinsic risk factors). If unwitnessed, assume syncope until proven otherwise.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - 12-lead ECG (looking for AF, heart block, LVH, long QT, acute ischaemia)
  - Capillary blood glucose
  - Orthostatic vitals (lying + standing at 1 and 3 minutes — drop in SBP ≥20 mmHg or DBP ≥10 mmHg)
  - Urinalysis (infection)
  - GCS and baseline cognitive screen (AMTS / 4AT)
- **Bloods (urgent):**
  - FBC, U&E, CRP, glucose, CK (if down time >1 hour)
  - Troponin (if chest pain, SOB, or syncope)
  - Consider thyroid function and vitamin B12 if first presentation of cognitive impairment
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** CT head if on anticoagulation / antiplatelet, GCS <15, focal neurology, witnessed LOC, or >65 with diffuse headache (NICE CG176)
  - **Second-line:** C-spine XR (if no high-risk mechanism and normal neuro exam) or CT C-spine (if high-risk or inadequate XR). Follow [local guidelines for clearance]
- **Special tests:**
  - ECG telemetry if suspected arrhythmia (monitor for ≥24h)
  - Carotid sinus massage only under specialist guidance
  - Echocardiogram if syncope with murmur / LVH / arrhythmia
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - NICE fall-risk assessment: ≥65 with one or more falls in past year → full multifactorial assessment
- **Never-miss test:** **ECG** — every fall in an older adult, even if mechanical cause suspected. A normal ECG does not rule out arrhythmia, but an abnormal one changes management immediately.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **ABC assessment** — manage airway if GCS <8, immobilise C-spine if any concern
  - **Treat injuries:** analgesia for fractures; wound care for lacerations; sutures/steri-strips if needed
  - **IV fluids** if hypotensive, septic, or prolonged lie (1L 0.9% saline stat, then reassess)
  - **Reverse anticoagulation** if confirmed intracranial haemorrhage — prothrombin complex concentrate or idarucizumab per [local protocol]
  - **Admit** if head injury on anticoagulation, new fracture requiring surgery, GCS <15, or unable to mobilise safely
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - **Full multifactorial falls assessment:** history of falls, gait/balance, muscle strength, home hazards, feet/footwear, vision, medications review
  - **Postural blood pressures** — repeat morning and evening for 3 days
  - **Bone protection:** check vitamin D and calcium; prescribe bisphosphonate if osteoporotic fracture (see Drugs)
  - **Hip protectors** if high risk of further falls with hip fracture
  - **Physiotherapy review** for mobility aid and home safety assessment
  - **Referral to falls clinic** or geriatrician for ongoing management
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Community falls prevention programme (strength and balance exercises)
  - Home hazard reduction (e.g., grab rails, non-slip mats, high-seat chairs)
  - Review all medications — stop or reduce sedatives, antihypertensives if postural hypotension
  - Vitamin D and calcium supplementation if deficient
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** 1L 0.9% saline/8h then reassess (or as per renal function)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40mg SC once daily) if immobile and no contraindication
  - **Diet:** full diet unless concern for swallowing; keep well hydrated
  - **Observations:** NEWS 4-hourly initially; after 24h can reduce to 8-hourly if stable

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line for fracture prevention:**
  - **Alendronic acid** 70mg PO once weekly (take with full glass of water, sit upright for ≥30 min after dose)
  - **Calcium + vitamin D** (e.g., Adcal D3 1 tab twice daily) — prescribe with any bisphosphonate
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Risedronate 35mg PO once weekly [check local policy]
  - Denosumab 60mg SC every 6 months (if intolerant to bisphosphonates)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Avoid bisphosphonates in eGFR <30, hypocalcaemia, oesophageal stricture
  - Do not give oral bisphosphonate with food, iron, calcium, or coffee within 30 min
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Alendronic acid: not recommended if CrCl <35 mL/min
  - Denosumab: no renal dose reduction, but monitor calcium closely in severe renal impairment
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Bisphosphonates** — giving with food or bedtime; can cause severe oesophagitis. Remind patient: “Swallow whole with plain water first thing in morning, sit up for 30 min, no breakfast yet.”

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Hip fracture (most common serious injury)
  - Intracranial haemorrhage (especially if on anticoagulants)
  - Cervical spine fracture
  - Rhabdomyolysis (AKI, hyperkalemia, cardiac arrest)
  - Wound infection / pressure sores from prolonged lie
- **Chronic:**
  - Fear of falling → activity restriction → deconditioning → further falls
  - Loss of independence → institutionalisation
  - Osteoporotic fractures (hip, wrist, vertebral)

### Prognosis
- 30% of older adults who fall in hospital will fall again within 6 months. 20% of hip fracture patients die within 1 year. Early multifactorial assessment reduces fall risk by 25%.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Check orthostatic vitals** on every older adult who falls — simple, cheap, and reveals a treatable cause.
- **Always ask “did you trip or lose consciousness?”** — if unclear, assume syncope and do a cardiac workup.
- **Review the medication chart** — especially antihypertensives, sedatives, anticholinergics — and consider deprescribing.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not label a fall as “mechanical”** without ruling out syncope — missing an arrhythmia or TIA is a missed opportunity for secondary prevention.
- **Do not assume a normal CT head rules out subdural** — acute subdurals can evolve over hours to days; repeat CT if GCS drops.
- **Do not send patient home** without checking safety — if they cannot walk independently, they need supervision; arrange a home care package first.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - New or worsening headache, confusion, or vomiting (suggests delayed intracranial bleed)
  - Unable to weight-bear or severe pain (undiagnosed fracture)
  - Any further fall within 24 hours
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP within 1 week for medication review and falls assessment
  - Outpatient falls clinic referral (if available) for comprehensive balance and strength programme

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / crash team if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (or single parameter ≥3)
  - GCS drop by ≥2 points or new focal neurology
  - Hypotension unresponsive to 1L bolus (MAP <65)
  - ECG showing new arrhythmia (AF with rapid rate, heart block, VT)
  - Concern for spinal cord compression (sensory level, acute urinary retention, loss of rectal tone)
